1. package tool
2. 
3. import (
4. 	"context"
5. 	"encoding/json"
6. 	"fmt"
7. 	"math/rand"
8. 	"os"
9. 	"os/exec"
10. 	"path/filepath"
11. 	"strings"
12. 	"time"
13. )
14. 
15. // EnterWorktree creates a new git worktree for isolated work.
16. type EnterWorktree struct {
17. 	WorkingDir string
18. }
19. 
20. func (t EnterWorktree) Name() string { return "enter_worktree" }
21. 
22. func (t EnterWorktree) Description() string {
23. 	return "Create an isolated git worktree. Returns the path to the new worktree directory. " +
24. 		"Use this when the user explicitly asks to work in a worktree, or when you need to test changes " +
25. 		"without affecting the current working directory. The worktree is created under .ggcode/worktrees/ " +
26. 		"with a new branch from HEAD. After creation, use the returned path as the working_dir for file " +
27. 		"and command operations to work inside the worktree."
28. }
29. 
30. func (t EnterWorktree) Parameters() json.RawMessage {
31. 	return json.RawMessage(`{
32. 	"type": "object",
33. 	"properties": {
34. 		"name": {
35. 			"type": "string",
36. 			"description": "Name for the worktree (used as directory and branch name). Defaults to a random name."
37. 		},
38. 		"description": {
39. 			"type": "string",
40. 			"description": "REQUIRED. Brief activity label shown in the UI. Write in the user's language (e.g. 'Searching for TODO patterns', '检查构建配置'). You MUST always provide this field."
41. 		}
42. 	},
43. 	"required": [
44. 		"description"
45. 	]
46. }`)
47. }
48. 
49. func (t EnterWorktree) Execute(ctx context.Context, input json.RawMessage) (Result, error) {
50. 	var args struct {
51. 		Name string `json:"name"`
52. 	}
53. 	if err := json.Unmarshal(input, &args); err != nil {
54. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("invalid input: %v", err)}, nil
55. 	}
56. 
57. 	name := args.Name
58. 	if name == "" {
59. 		// Generate a random-ish name
60. 		name = fmt.Sprintf("wt-%s-%04d", time.Now().Format("20060102"), rand.Intn(10000))
61. 	}
62. 
63. 	// Sanitize name: only allow safe characters
64. 	for _, c := range name {
65. 		if !isWorktreeNameChar(c) {
66. 			return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("invalid worktree name %q: only letters, digits, dots, underscores, and dashes allowed", name)}, nil
67. 		}
68. 	}
69. 
70. 	// Find git root
71. 	gitRoot, err := findGitRoot(ctx, t.WorkingDir)
72. 	if err != nil {
73. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("not a git repository: %v", err)}, nil
74. 	}
75. 
76. 	worktreesDir := filepath.Join(gitRoot, ".ggcode", "worktrees")
77. 	if err := os.MkdirAll(worktreesDir, 0755); err != nil {
78. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error creating worktrees dir: %v", err)}, nil
79. 	}
80. 
81. 	worktreePath := filepath.Join(worktreesDir, name)
82. 	branchName := name
83. 
84. 	// Create worktree with new branch from HEAD
85. 	cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "git", "worktree", "add", "-b", branchName, worktreePath, "HEAD")
86. 	cmd.Dir = gitRoot
87. 	cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GIT_PAGER=cat")
88. 	out, err := cmd.CombinedOutput()
89. 	if err != nil {
90. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error creating worktree: %s", strings.TrimSpace(string(out)))}, nil
91. 	}
92. 
93. 	return Result{
94. 		Content:             fmt.Sprintf("Created worktree at %s (branch: %s). All subsequent tool calls will use this directory.", worktreePath, branchName),
95. 		SuggestedWorkingDir: worktreePath,
96. 	}, nil
97. }
98. 
99. // ExitWorktree exits and optionally removes a git worktree.
100. type ExitWorktree struct {
101. 	WorkingDir string
102. }
103. 
104. func (t ExitWorktree) Name() string { return "exit_worktree" }
105. 
106. func (t ExitWorktree) Description() string {
107. 	return "Exit and optionally remove a git worktree. " +
108. 		"Use this when the user asks to exit a worktree session. " +
109. 		"'keep' leaves the worktree directory and branch intact for later use. " +
110. 		"'remove' deletes the worktree directory and its branch. " +
111. 		"If there are uncommitted changes, you must set discard_changes=true or the removal will be rejected."
112. }
113. 
114. func (t ExitWorktree) Parameters() json.RawMessage {
115. 	return json.RawMessage(`{
116. 	"type": "object",
117. 	"properties": {
118. 		"action": {
119. 			"type": "string",
120. 			"enum": [
121. 				"keep",
122. 				"remove"
123. 			],
124. 			"description": "'keep' leaves the worktree directory and branch intact. 'remove' deletes both."
125. 		},
126. 		"discard_changes": {
127. 			"type": "boolean",
128. 			"default": false,
129. 			"description": "If true, discard uncommitted changes when removing. Required when there are uncommitted changes."
130. 		},
131. 		"description": {
132. 			"type": "string",
133. 			"description": "REQUIRED. Brief activity label shown in the UI. Write in the user's language (e.g. 'Searching for TODO patterns', '检查构建配置'). You MUST always provide this field."
134. 		}
135. 	},
136. 	"required": [
137. 		"action",
138. 		"description"
139. 	]
140. }`)
141. }
142. 
143. func (t ExitWorktree) Execute(ctx context.Context, input json.RawMessage) (Result, error) {
144. 	var args struct {
145. 		Action         string `json:"action"`
146. 		DiscardChanges bool   `json:"discard_changes"`
147. 	}
148. 	if err := json.Unmarshal(input, &args); err != nil {
149. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("invalid input: %v", err)}, nil
150. 	}
151. 
152. 	// Find git root
153. 	gitRoot, err := findGitRoot(ctx, t.WorkingDir)
154. 	if err != nil {
155. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("not a git repository: %v", err)}, nil
156. 	}
157. 
158. 	// Check if we're actually in a worktree
159. 	isWorktree, worktreePath, err := isInsideWorktree(gitRoot)
160. 	if err != nil {
161. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error checking worktree status: %v", err)}, nil
162. 	}
163. 	if !isWorktree {
164. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: "not currently inside a worktree created by enter_worktree"}, nil
165. 	}
166. 
167. 	// Find the main repo root so we can suggest switching back
168. 	mainRepoRoot, _ := findGitRootFromWorktree(worktreePath)
169. 
170. 	if args.Action == "keep" {
171. 		return Result{
172. 			Content:             fmt.Sprintf("Worktree at %s kept intact.", worktreePath),
173. 			SuggestedWorkingDir: mainRepoRoot,
174. 		}, nil
175. 	}
176. 
177. 	// action == "remove"
178. 	// Check for uncommitted changes
179. 	cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "git", "status", "--porcelain")
180. 	cmd.Dir = gitRoot
181. 	cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GIT_PAGER=cat")
182. 	out, err := cmd.Output()
183. 	if err != nil {
184. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error checking git status: %v", err)}, nil
185. 	}
186. 	if len(strings.TrimSpace(string(out))) > 0 && !args.DiscardChanges {
187. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: "worktree has uncommitted changes. Set discard_changes=true to remove anyway, or commit/stash your changes first."}, nil
188. 	}
189. 
190. 	// Get branch name before removing
191. 	branchCmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "git", "branch", "--show-current")
192. 	branchCmd.Dir = gitRoot
193. 	branchCmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GIT_PAGER=cat")
194. 	branchOut, _ := branchCmd.Output()
195. 	branchName := strings.TrimSpace(string(branchOut))
196. 
197. 	// Remove the worktree
198. 	rmCmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "git", "worktree", "remove", "--force", worktreePath)
199. 	if mainRepoRoot != "" {
200. 		rmCmd.Dir = mainRepoRoot
201. 	} else {
202. 		rmCmd.Dir = filepath.Dir(filepath.Dir(worktreePath))
203. 	}
204. 	rmCmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GIT_PAGER=cat")
205. 	if rmOut, err := rmCmd.CombinedOutput(); err != nil {
206. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error removing worktree: %s", strings.TrimSpace(string(rmOut)))}, nil
207. 	}
208. 
209. 	// Optionally delete the branch
210. 	if branchName != "" && branchName != "main" && branchName != "master" {
211. 		delCmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "git", "branch", "-D", branchName)
212. 		delCmd.Dir = mainRepoRoot
213. 		delCmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GIT_PAGER=cat")
214. 		_ = delCmd.Run() // best effort
215. 	}
216. 
217. 	return Result{
218. 		Content:             fmt.Sprintf("Removed worktree %s", worktreePath),
219. 		SuggestedWorkingDir: mainRepoRoot,
220. 	}, nil
221. }
222. 
223. // isWorktreeNameChar returns true for characters allowed in worktree names.
224. func isWorktreeNameChar(c rune) bool {
225. 	return (c >= 'a' && c <= 'z') || (c >= 'A' && c <= 'Z') || (c >= '0' && c <= '9') || c == '-' || c == '_' || c == '.'
226. }
227. 
228. // findGitRoot finds the git repository root from a directory.
229. func findGitRoot(ctx context.Context, dir string) (string, error) {
230. 	cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, "git", "rev-parse", "--show-toplevel")
231. 	cmd.Dir = dir
232. 	cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GIT_PAGER=cat")
233. 	out, err := cmd.Output()
234. 	if err != nil {
235. 		return "", fmt.Errorf("not a git repository")
236. 	}
237. 	return strings.TrimSpace(string(out)), nil
238. }
239. 
240. // isInsideWorktree checks if the current directory is inside a .ggcode/worktrees subdirectory.
```

252. // toolWorkingDirMu is a safety-net mutex for syncToolWorkingDir. With Registry.Clone(),
253. // each agent has its own tool instances and this mutex should never be contended.
254. // It exists as a last resort in case a tool without a Clone() implementation is
255. // shared between agents and has a WorkingDir field that needs mutation.
256. var toolWorkingDirMu sync.Mutex
257. 
258. // syncToolWorkingDir uses reflection to set the WorkingDir field on tools
259. // that have one. This ensures tools always use the agent's current working
260. // directory, even after it changes (e.g., after enter_worktree).
261. //
262. // Note: With Registry.Clone(), each agent has independent tool instances, so
263. // this reflection is only mutating per-agent copies. The mutex is a safety net.
264. func syncToolWorkingDir(t tool.Tool, dir string) {
265. 	toolWorkingDirMu.Lock()
266. 	defer toolWorkingDirMu.Unlock()
267. 
268. 	// Dereference pointer if needed
269. 	v := reflect.ValueOf(t)
270. 	if v.Kind() == reflect.Ptr {
271. 		if v.IsNil() {
272. 			return
273. 		}
274. 		v = v.Elem()
275. 	}
276. 	if v.Kind() != reflect.Struct {
277. 		return
278. 	}
279. 	f := v.FieldByName("WorkingDir")
280. 	if f.IsValid() && f.CanSet() && f.Kind() == reflect.String {
281. 		f.SetString(dir)
282. 	}
283. }
284.
```

166. // TestAllWorkingDirToolsImplementCloner is a compile-time/runtime safety check:
167. // every tool type that has a WorkingDir field MUST implement the Cloner interface.
168. // If someone adds a new tool with WorkingDir but forgets Clone(), this test fails.
169. func TestAllWorkingDirToolsImplementCloner(t *testing.T) {
170. 	// List of tool instances that have WorkingDir — must all implement Cloner
171. 	toolsWithWorkingDir := []Tool{
172. 		&RunCommand{},
173. 		&GitStatus{},
174. 		&GitDiff{},
175. 		&GitLog{},
176. 		&GitShow{},
177. 		&GitBlame{},

187. 		SkillTool{},
188. 	}
189. 
190. 	for _, t2 := range toolsWithWorkingDir {
191. 		if _, ok := t2.(Cloner); !ok {
192. 			t.Errorf("%T has WorkingDir but does not implement Cloner — add Clone() Tool method", t2)
193. 		}
194. 	}
195. }
196.

1. package permission
2. 
3. import (
4. 	"encoding/json"
5. 	"strings"
6. 	"sync"
7. 
8. 	"github.com/topcheer/ggcode/internal/config"
9. 	"github.com/topcheer/ggcode/internal/debug"
10. 	"github.com/topcheer/ggcode/internal/util"
11. )
12. 
13. // DefaultMode is the default permission mode if not specified.
14. var DefaultMode = SupervisedMode
15. 
16. // ToolRule defines the permission level for a tool.
17. type ToolRule struct {
18. 	Decision Decision `yaml:"decision"`
19. }
20. 
21. // ConfigPolicy implements PermissionPolicy based on configuration rules.
22. type ConfigPolicy struct {
23. 	rules           map[string]Decision
24. 	sandbox         *PathSandbox
25. 	readOnlySandbox *PathSandbox
26. 	detector        *DangerousDetector
27. 	mode            PermissionMode
28. 	mu              sync.RWMutex
29. }
30. 
31. // NewConfigPolicy creates a policy from tool rules and allowed directories.
32. // Default decision is Ask for any tool not explicitly listed.
33. func NewConfigPolicy(rules map[string]Decision, allowedDirs []string) *ConfigPolicy {
34. 	return NewConfigPolicyWithMode(rules, allowedDirs, DefaultMode)
35. }
36. 
37. // NewConfigPolicyWithMode creates a policy with an explicit permission mode.
38. func NewConfigPolicyWithMode(rules map[string]Decision, allowedDirs []string, mode PermissionMode) *ConfigPolicy {
39. 	return NewConfigPolicyWithModeAndReadOnlyDirs(rules, allowedDirs, nil, mode)
40. }
41. 
42. // NewConfigPolicyWithModeAndReadOnlyDirs creates a policy with optional
43. // read-only file access outside the main writable sandbox.
44. func NewConfigPolicyWithModeAndReadOnlyDirs(rules map[string]Decision, allowedDirs, readOnlyDirs []string, mode PermissionMode) *ConfigPolicy {
45. 	if rules == nil {
46. 		rules = make(map[string]Decision)
47. 	}
48. 	return &ConfigPolicy{
49. 		rules:           rules,
50. 		sandbox:         NewPathSandbox(allowedDirs),
51. 		readOnlySandbox: newOptionalPathSandbox(readOnlyDirs),
52. 		detector:        NewDangerousDetector(),
53. 		mode:            mode,
54. 	}
55. }
56. 
57. // Check returns the permission decision for a tool call.
58. func (p *ConfigPolicy) Check(toolName string, input json.RawMessage) (Decision, error) {
59. 	debug.Log("permission", "Check: tool=%s input=%s", toolName, truncateStr(string(input), 200))
60. 	p.mu.RLock()
61. 	defer p.mu.RUnlock()
62. 
63. 	// Mode-specific handling
64. 	switch p.mode {
65. 	case BypassMode, AutopilotMode:
66. 		// Bypass mode: allow everything except extremely dangerous operations
67. 		if isCommandTool(toolName) {
68. 			cmd, _ := extractCommand(input)
69. 			if cmd != "" && p.detector.IsExtremelyDangerous(cmd) {
70. 				return Ask, nil
71. 			}
72. 		}
73. 		// Check sandbox for file tools (still protect workspace boundary).
74. 		// In bypass/autopilot, any *write* outside the writable sandbox is
75. 		// downgraded to Ask, regardless of whether the path is on the small
76. 		// "sensitive" allow-list. Without this, ~/.aws/credentials,
77. 		// ~/.docker/config.json, /etc/**, and arbitrary user files outside
78. 		// the workspace get silently overwritten on a prompt-injected tool
79. 		// call. See locks.md S5.
80. 		if isWriteFileTool(toolName) {
81. 			path, _ := extractFilePath(input)
82. 			if path != "" && !p.sandbox.Allowed(path) {
83. 				return Ask, nil
84. 			}
85. 		} else if isFileTool(toolName) {
86. 			path, _ := extractFilePath(input)
87. 			if path != "" && !p.sandbox.Allowed(path) && isSensitivePath(path) {
88. 				return Ask, nil
89. 			}
90. 		}
91. 		return Allow, nil
92. 	case PlanMode:
93. 		// Plan mode: mode control tools + read-only tools allowed, everything else denied
94. 		// enter_plan_mode is always allowed (entering read-only mode is safe).
95. 		// exit_plan_mode requires user confirmation in supervised mode — the plan
96. 		// determines what code changes the agent will make next.
97. 		if toolName == "enter_plan_mode" {
98. 			return Allow, nil
99. 		}
100. 		if toolName == "exit_plan_mode" {
101. 			return Allow, nil
102. 		}
103. 		if IsReadOnlyTool(toolName) {
104. 			// Read-only tools are always allowed in plan mode, even outside sandbox.
105. 			// Plan mode is strictly read-only, so there's no risk.
106. 			return Allow, nil
107. 		}
108. 		return Deny, nil
109. 	case AutoMode:
110. 		// Auto mode: allow safe ops, deny dangerous ones, no prompts
111. 		if isCommandTool(toolName) {
112. 			cmd, _ := extractCommand(input)
113. 			if cmd != "" && p.detector.IsDangerous(cmd) {
114. 				return Deny, nil
115. 			}
116. 		}
117. 		// Check sandbox for file tools
118. 		if isFileTool(toolName) {
119. 			path, _ := extractFilePath(input)
120. 			if path != "" && !p.sandbox.Allowed(path) {
121. 				return Deny, nil
122. 			}
123. 		}
124. 		return Allow, nil
125. 	}
126. 
127. 	// Supervised mode (default): check overrides, then ask
128. 	if d, ok := p.rules[toolName]; ok {
129. 		if isFileTool(toolName) {
130. 			path, _ := extractFilePath(input)
131. 			if path != "" && !p.sandbox.Allowed(path) {
132. 				return Deny, nil
133. 			}
134. 		}
135. 		if isCommandTool(toolName) {
136. 			cmd, _ := extractCommand(input)
137. 			if cmd != "" && p.detector.IsDangerous(cmd) {
138. 				return Deny, nil
139. 			}
140. 		}
141. 		return d, nil
142. 	}
143. 
144. 	return Ask, nil
145. }
146. 
147. // IsDangerous returns true if the command is inherently dangerous.
148. func (p *ConfigPolicy) IsDangerous(command string) bool {
149. 	return p.detector.IsDangerous(command)
150. }
151. 
152. // AllowedPath returns true if the path is within the sandbox.
153. func (p *ConfigPolicy) AllowedPath(path string) bool {
154. 	if p.sandbox.Allowed(path) {
155. 		return true
156. 	}
157. 	return p.readOnlySandbox != nil && p.readOnlySandbox.Allowed(path)
158. }
159. 
160. // AllowedPathForTool returns true if the path is allowed for the specific tool.
161. // In non-plan modes, if execution reaches here the permission layer has already
162. // approved the tool call (either Allow directly or user approved an Ask), so
163. // sandbox restrictions are lifted. In PlanMode, strict sandbox enforcement
164. // applies since plan mode never writes outside the workspace.
165. func (p *ConfigPolicy) AllowedPathForTool(toolName, path string) bool {
166. 	if p.sandbox.Allowed(path) {
167. 		return true
168. 	}
169. 	// Non-plan modes: permission layer already approved, allow execution
170. 	p.mu.RLock()
171. 	isPlan := p.mode == PlanMode
172. 	p.mu.RUnlock()
173. 	if !isPlan {
174. 		return true
175. 	}
176. 	// Plan mode: read-only tools bypass sandbox (no risk since they can't write)
177. 	if isReadOnlyFileTool(toolName) {
178. 		return true
179. 	}

"*** Begin Patch\n*** Update File: /Users/zhanju/.openclaw/workspace-teamclaw/projects/https-github-com-topcheer-cc-git-ggcode-coding-age-s7ccb6/docs/plans/2026-05-20-multi-file-edit-design.md\n@@\n The two tools are designed as a pair. `multi_file_read` optimizes context collection; `multi_file_edit` optimizes coordinated writes.\n+\n+## Protocol clarifications\n+\n+These points must be fixed in the design so implementation and agent behavior do not drift:\n+\n+- `multi_file_read` and `multi_file_edit` both require absolute paths.\n+- Paths must be normalized with `filepath.Clean` before duplicate detection, sandbox checks, reads, writes, and reporting.\n+- Duplicate-path checks operate on the cleaned absolute path string.\n+- v1 does not attempt symlink canonicalization beyond `filepath.Clean`; callers should not provide the same target through multiple symlink aliases.\n+- `multi_file_edit` only supports plain text files that can be written back directly. It does not support editing extracted document formats such as PDF, DOCX, or EPUB.\n+- `multi_file_read` may support text files plus extracted document text, but only its plain text output is valid input to `multi_file_edit`.\n+- To preserve workflow parity, `multi_file_edit` matching must auto-strip `multi_file_read` wrapper lines if they are accidentally pasted into `old_text` or `new_text`. This includes:\n+  - `[multi_file_read summary] ...`\n+  - `=== FILE: ... ===`\n+  - `=== ERROR: ... ===`\n+  - `[end file]`\n+  - `[end error]`\n+  - `[skipped: ...]`\n@@\n-## Proposed tool shape\n+## Proposed `multi_file_edit` shape\n@@\n-If the request would exceed those limits, the tool should fail fast and tell the model to split the read into smaller batches or narrower ranges.\n+Static request-limit violations should fail fast and tell the model to split the read into smaller batches or narrower ranges.\n \n This is intentionally stricter than `read_file`. The point of `multi_file_read` is efficient context gathering, not dumping large files wholesale.\n@@\n 1. Validate top-level input before reading:\n    - non-empty `files`\n    - unique `path`\n    - request stays within batch limits\n 2. For each file:\n    - run the shared single-file reader\n    - append either a success block or an error block\n-3. Stop before exceeding the combined output limit and return a clear truncation/split-batch notice if necessary\n+3. If the runtime combined output limit is reached:\n+   - stop appending further file bodies\n+   - append explicit skipped markers for the remaining paths\n+   - append a clear split-batch notice in the summary\n \n Unlike `multi_file_edit`, `multi_file_read` should always be best-effort per file. Read failures in one path should not block the others, because partial read success is still useful for planning.\n@@\n [multi_file_read summary] requested=3 succeeded=2 failed=1\n \n === FILE: /repo/a.go ===\n [indent: tab]\n@@\n === ERROR: /repo/missing.go ===\n error reading file: open /repo/missing.go: no such file or directory\n [end error]\n+\n+=== FILE: /repo/too-many.go ===\n+[skipped: combined output limit reached; split into a smaller batch]\n+[end file]\n ```\n@@\n - preserve current metadata headers when available\n - keep output order identical to input order\n+- include explicit skipped markers when later files were omitted due to output budget\n \n This makes the output easy for the LLM to read and easy to copy from into `multi_file_edit`.\n@@\n ```json\n {\n-  \"summary\": \"Applied edits to 5 files: 4 succeeded, 1 failed\",\n+  \"summary\": \"Requested 5 files: 3 written, 1 failed, 1 skipped\",\n   \"mode\": \"atomic\",\n   \"applied\": false,\n-  \"succeeded_files\": 4,\n+  \"planned_files\": 4,\n+  \"written_files\": 0,\n   \"failed_files\": 1,\n+  \"skipped_files\": 3,\n   \"written_paths\": [],\n   \"failed_paths\": [\"/repo/b.go\"],\n+  \"skipped_paths\": [\"/repo/a.go\", \"/repo/c.go\", \"/repo/d.go\"],\n   \"results\": [\n     {\n       \"path\": \"/repo/a.go\",\n       \"status\": \"planned\",\n@@\n ```\n \n `results[]` order should match input order.\n \n Status meanings:\n \n - `success`: file was written\n - `planned`: file would have succeeded, but atomic mode skipped writes after another failure\n+- `skipped`: file was not attempted or not written because the tool stopped before execution\n - `error`: file planning or writing failed\n \n This JSON-in-`Content` approach gives the LLM machine-stable fields without requiring a change to the current tool result contract.\n+\n+### `IsError` semantics\n+\n+`Result.IsError` must be defined explicitly:\n+\n+- `multi_file_read`\n+  - `IsError = true` only for top-level malformed requests that produce no useful per-file output, such as invalid JSON, duplicate cleaned paths, or static batch-limit violations.\n+  - `IsError = false` for mixed read outcomes, including when some files fail or some later files are skipped due to runtime output limits.\n+- `multi_file_edit`\n+  - `IsError = false` only when every requested file is written successfully.\n+  - `IsError = true` in atomic mode if any file fails and no writes are applied.\n+  - `IsError = true` in `partial_success` mode if any requested file fails, even if some files were already written.\n+\n+This preserves useful payloads while still signaling to the agent that user-visible recovery is required.\n@@\n Top-level validation should still fail fast for malformed requests, such as duplicate paths or batch-limit violations.\n@@\n ## Registration and UX\n \n - Add `MultiFileRead` and `MultiFileEdit` to `internal/tool/builtin.go`.\n+- Register `MultiFileRead` with the other file read/search tools and `MultiFileEdit` with the other mutating file tools so the built-in tool set remains easy to reason about.\n - `multi_file_read` description should explicitly recommend:\n   - requesting only the files and ranges needed for the current task\n   - keeping ranges narrow on large files\n   - copying numbered lines directly into `multi_file_edit`\n@@\n   - keeping batches small\n   - relying on the default atomic mode unless mixed success is explicitly acceptable\n - Parameter descriptions should mirror the proven language from `edit_file` / `multi_edit_file`.\n+\n+## Agent, permission, and multi-agent integration\n+\n+The design must also specify the integration-layer work needed beyond implementing the tools themselves.\n+\n+### Tool registration\n+\n+- Add `MultiFileRead{SandboxCheck: sandboxFor(\"multi_file_read\")}` and `MultiFileEdit{SandboxCheck: sandboxFor(\"multi_file_edit\")}` to `internal/tool/builtin.go`.\n+- Update any built-in tool documentation or release notes that enumerate file tools.\n+- Update config/documentation examples for `tool_permissions` so the new tool names are visible to users.\n+\n+### Permission classification\n+\n+The permission layer currently classifies file tools by name and extracts only a single path from tool input. That is insufficient for batched file tools.\n+\n+Implementation must:\n+\n+- classify `multi_file_read` as a read-only file tool in plan mode\n+- classify `multi_file_edit` as a write file tool\n+- extend the permission path extractor to understand `files[].path`\n+- apply sandbox/path checks to every cleaned path in the request, not just the first one\n+\n+Without this, plan mode, auto mode, and bypass/autopilot sandbox enforcement will be incomplete for batched tools.\n+\n+### File diff preview and checkpoints\n+\n+The current agent special-cases only `edit_file` and `write_file` for diff preview and checkpoint persistence. `multi_file_edit` must not silently bypass that behavior.\n+\n+Integration should therefore either:\n+\n+- generalize the current single-file flow into a multi-file-aware diff/checkpoint path, or\n+- add a dedicated `executeMultiFileTool` path that computes diffs per file before writing\n+\n+The preferred behavior is:\n+\n+- show one combined confirmation flow containing per-file diffs when confirmation is enabled\n+- persist checkpoints for each successfully written file\n+- skip checkpoint writes for planned/skipped/error files\n+\n+### WorkingDir and multi-agent isolation\n+\n+This is the most important integration constraint:\n+\n+- `multi_file_read` and `multi_file_edit` should be implemented as stateless tools with **no `WorkingDir` field**.\n+- They should require absolute paths and rely only on `SandboxCheck`.\n+- They should never set `SuggestedWorkingDir`.\n+\n+Because they have no mutable `WorkingDir`, they are safe to share across agents and do not need `Clone()`.\n+\n+If a future revision adds `WorkingDir` or relative-path semantics, then it MUST also:\n+\n+- implement `Clone() Tool`\n+- be covered by `TestAllWorkingDirToolsImplementCloner`\n+- remain compatible with `syncToolWorkingDir`\n+\n+This is required so sub-agents and worktree-based agents do not overwrite each other's effective working directory.\n \n ## Testing\n \n Add tests for:\n@@\n 1. top-level validation\n    - empty files array\n    - duplicate path\n+   - non-absolute path\n    - unsupported mode\n    - request exceeds limits\n 2. multi-file read behavior\n    - multiple file blocks are returned in input order\n    - per-file offset/limit is respected\n    - read errors are isolated to the failing path\n-   - combined output limit is enforced\n+   - combined output limit is enforced with explicit skipped markers\n    - numbered lines and metadata headers match `read_file`\n 3. atomic default behavior\n    - one file succeeds, one fails, no file is written\n    - successful files are reported as `planned`\n@@\n 7. result content format\n    - `multi_file_read` block delimiters are stable\n    - `multi_file_edit` `Content` is valid JSON\n-   - `written_paths` / `failed_paths` match actual writes\n+   - `planned_files` / `written_files` / `failed_files` / `skipped_files` are internally consistent\n+   - `written_paths` / `failed_paths` / `skipped_paths` match actual writes\n+   - `IsError` matches the documented mode semantics\n 8. registration\n     - built-in registry exposes both `multi_file_read` and `multi_file_edit`\n+   - permission mode classification includes both tools\n 9. workflow parity\n    - content copied from `multi_file_read` can be used directly as anchors in `multi_file_edit`\n+   - `multi_file_read` wrapper lines pasted into `multi_file_edit` are ignored correctly\n+10. integration behavior\n+   - multi-file edit participates in diff preview/checkpoint flow\n+   - multi-agent/worktree tests confirm no working-directory cross-talk\n*** End Patch\n"
````

283. 
284. Design constraints:
285. 
286. - keep the existing numbered-line format unchanged inside each file block
287. - use deterministic file/error delimiters
288. - preserve current metadata headers when available
289. - keep output order identical to input order
290. - include explicit skipped markers when later files were omitted due to output budget
291. 
292. This makes the output easy for the LLM to read and easy to copy from into `multi_file_edit`.
293. 
294. ### `multi_file_edit`
295. 
296. The current tool framework returns `Result.Content string`, not a native structured payload. So the tool should return a stable JSON string in `Content`, with a short human-readable summary duplicated inside that JSON.
297. 
298. Suggested `Content` payload:
299. 
300. ```json
301. {
302.   "summary": "Requested 5 files: 3 written, 1 failed, 1 skipped",
303.   "mode": "atomic",
304.   "applied": false,
305.   "planned_files": 4,
306.   "written_files": 0,
307.   "failed_files": 1,
308.   "skipped_files": 3,
309.   "written_paths": [],
310.   "failed_paths": ["/repo/b.go"],
311.   "skipped_paths": ["/repo/a.go", "/repo/c.go", "/repo/d.go"],
312.   "results": [
313.     {
314.       "path": "/repo/a.go",
315.       "status": "planned",
316.       "applied_edit_count": 2
317.     },
318.     {
319.       "path": "/repo/b.go",
320.       "status": "error",
321.       "error": "edits[1]: old_text not found in file"
322.     }
323.   ]
324. }
325. ```
326. 
327. `results[]` order should match input order.
328. 
329. Status meanings:
330.
````

<summary>420 lines</summary>

````
1. # Multi-file read/edit tool design
2. 
3. ## Summary
4. 
5. Add two new built-in tools:
6. 
7. - `multi_file_read` for batching reads across multiple existing files in one tool call
8. - `multi_file_edit` for batching edits across multiple existing files in one tool call
9. 
10. The first version is intentionally narrow:
11. 
12. - Only works on existing files.
13. - Reuses current `read_file`, `edit_file`, and `multi_edit_file` semantics as much as possible.
14. - Uses grouped `files[]` inputs for both read and edit tools.
15. - Defaults to **atomic** cross-file behavior for easier LLM recovery.
16. - Optionally supports `mode: "partial_success"` for callers that prefer best-effort writes.
17. 
18. This keeps the tools easy for weaker models to use while avoiding a brand-new matching model.
19. 
20. ## Goals
21. 
22. - Reduce round trips when one task requires coordinated edits in several files.
23. - Reuse the already-hardened matching behavior from `edit_file` / `multi_edit_file`, including:
24.   - read_file numbered-line anchors
25.   - wrapper-line stripping
26.   - indentation normalization
27.   - CRLF tolerance
28.   - leading-indent shift fallback
29. - Preserve the same line-numbered read format that powers the current edit workflow.
30. - Return structured per-file results so the agent can retry only failures.
31. - Keep the tool request small and predictable enough for weaker models to call reliably.
32. - Provide a complete multi-file read + write loop rather than only optimizing the write side.
33. 
34. ## Non-goals
35. 
36. - Creating new files.
37. - Overwriting files wholesale like `write_file`.
38. - Replacing `read_file` / `edit_file` for single-file use.
39. - Supporting repeated `path` entries in one call.
40. - Changing single-file edit semantics.
41. 
42. ## End-to-end workflow
43. 
44. The intended LLM workflow is:
45. 
46. 1. Use `multi_file_read` to fetch the relevant files or file ranges in one round trip.
47. 2. Inspect the numbered output and decide what to change.
48. 3. Use `multi_file_edit` with numbered `old_text` anchors copied from `multi_file_read`.
49. 4. If edit planning fails, re-read only the failed files or narrower ranges and retry.
50. 
51. The two tools are designed as a pair. `multi_file_read` optimizes context collection; `multi_file_edit` optimizes coordinated writes.
52. 
53. ## Protocol clarifications
54. 
55. These points must be fixed in the design so implementation and agent behavior do not drift:
56. 
57. - `multi_file_read` and `multi_file_edit` both require absolute paths.
58. - Paths must be normalized with `filepath.Clean` before duplicate detection, sandbox checks, reads, writes, and reporting.
59. - Duplicate-path checks operate on the cleaned absolute path string.
60. - v1 does not attempt symlink canonicalization beyond `filepath.Clean`; callers should not provide the same target through multiple symlink aliases.
61. - `multi_file_edit` only supports plain text files that can be written back directly. It does not support editing extracted document formats such as PDF, DOCX, or EPUB.
62. - `multi_file_read` may support text files plus extracted document text, but only its plain text output is valid input to `multi_file_edit`.
63. - To preserve workflow parity, `multi_file_edit` matching must auto-strip `multi_file_read` wrapper lines if they are accidentally pasted into `old_text` or `new_text`. This includes:
64.   - `[multi_file_read summary] ...`
65.   - `=== FILE: ... ===`
66.   - `=== ERROR: ... ===`
67.   - `[end file]`
68.   - `[end error]`
69.   - `[skipped: ...]`
70. 
71. ## Proposed `multi_file_read` shape
72. 
73. ```json
74. {
75.   "files": [
76.     {
77.       "path": "/repo/a.go",
78.       "offset": 1,
79.       "limit": 120
80.     },
81.     {
82.       "path": "/repo/b.go",
83.       "offset": 40,
84.       "limit": 80
85.     }
86.   ],
87.   "description": "Optional UI label"
88. }
89. ```
90. 
91. Rules:
92. 
93. - `files[].path` must be unique within one call.
94. - `offset` and `limit` follow the same semantics as `read_file`.
95. - Omitted `offset` means start from the beginning.
96. - Omitted `limit` means use the multi-file default per-file cap rather than the larger single-file cap.
97. - v1 should focus on text files and extracted document text. Image/binary batching is out of scope.
98. 
99. ## Proposed `multi_file_edit` shape
100. 
101. ```json
102. {
103.   "mode": "atomic",
104.   "files": [
105.     {
106.       "path": "/repo/a.go",
107.       "edits": [
108.         {
109.           "old_text": "     12\tfoo()",
110.           "new_text": "     12\tbar()"
111.         }
112.       ]
113.     },
114.     {
115.       "path": "/repo/b.go",
116.       "edits": [
117.         {
118.           "old_text": "x := 1",
119.           "new_text": "x := 2"
120.         }
121.       ]
122.     }
123.   ],
124.   "description": "Optional UI label"
125. }
126. ```
127. 
128. Rules:
129. 
130. - `files[].path` must be unique within one call.
131. - Each file's `edits` behaves exactly like `multi_edit_file`.
132. - If the same file needs several edits, they must be grouped into one `files[i].edits`.
133. - `mode` defaults to `"atomic"` when omitted.
134. - `mode: "partial_success"` is opt-in and should be used only when the caller is prepared to reconcile mixed file state.
135. 
136. ## Limits
137. 
138. The tools should be optimized for small, coordinated multi-file changes rather than giant repository-wide rewrites.
139. 
140. ### `multi_file_read` limits
141. 
142. Recommended v1 limits:
143. 
144. - at most 20 files per call
145. - default per-file cap: 200 lines when `limit` is omitted
146. - max explicit per-file `limit`: 500 lines
147. - max combined output: 4000 lines or 300 KB, whichever comes first
148. 
149. Static request-limit violations should fail fast and tell the model to split the read into smaller batches or narrower ranges.
150. 
151. This is intentionally stricter than `read_file`. The point of `multi_file_read` is efficient context gathering, not dumping large files wholesale.
152. 
153. ### `multi_file_edit` limits
154. 
155. Recommended v1 limits:
156. 
157. - at most 10 files per call
158. - at most 20 edits per file
159. - at most 200 total edits per call
160. - at most 200 KB combined `old_text` + `new_text` payload
161. 
162. Requests beyond those limits should fail fast with a clear message telling the model to split the batch.
163. 
164. ## Execution model
165. 
166. Implementation should be split into three layers.
167. 
168. ### 1. Shared single-file reader
169. 
170. Extract the core reading logic from `read_file` into a shared helper that:
171. 
172. - validates sandbox access
173. - reads the file
174. - preserves the current line-numbered output style
175. - preserves metadata hints such as indent or encoding headers
176. - applies the same range logic as `read_file`
177. - returns either:
178.   - a formatted read block
179.   - or a per-file read error
180. 
181. This helper must be reused by:
182. 
183. - `read_file`
184. - `multi_file_read`
185. 
186. That keeps output shape consistent so the model can copy numbered lines from either tool into edit calls.
187. 
188. ### 2. Shared single-file planner
189. 
190. Extract the core planning logic from `multi_edit_file` into a shared helper that:
191. 
192. - reads original content
193. - resolves every edit against that original content
194. - validates uniqueness and overlap
195. - computes the final new content
196. - returns either:
197.   - a write plan (`path`, `new_content`, `applied_edit_count`)
198.   - or a structured failure
199. 
200. This helper must be reused by:
201. 
202. - `multi_edit_file`
203. - `multi_file_edit`
204. 
205. That keeps matching behavior consistent and avoids future drift.
206. 
207. ### 3. Multi-file orchestrators
208. 
209. #### `multi_file_read`
210. 
211. `multi_file_read` should:
212. 
213. 1. Validate top-level input before reading:
214.    - non-empty `files`
215.    - unique `path`
216.    - request stays within batch limits
217. 2. For each file:
218.    - run the shared single-file reader
219.    - append either a success block or an error block
220. 3. If the runtime combined output limit is reached:
221.    - stop appending further file bodies
222.    - append explicit skipped markers for the remaining paths
223.    - append a clear split-batch notice in the summary
224. 
225. Unlike `multi_file_edit`, `multi_file_read` should always be best-effort per file. Read failures in one path should not block the others, because partial read success is still useful for planning.
226. 
227. #### `multi_file_edit`
228. 
229. `multi_file_edit` should:
230. 
231. 1. Validate top-level input before any write:
232.    - non-empty `files`
233.    - unique `path`
234.    - no empty `old_text`
235.    - sandbox permission for every path
236.    - request stays within batch limits
237. 2. For each file:
238.    - read file
239.    - run the shared planner
240.    - store either a pending write or a failure result
241. 3. If `mode == "atomic"`:
242.    - abort all writes if any file planning step failed
243.    - return all per-file failures plus any planned successes as skipped
244. 4. If `mode == "partial_success"`:
245.    - persist only the successful pending writes
246.    - return a mixed result covering both successes and failures
247. 
248. This default matters for LLM efficiency. Atomic mode is easier for models to recover from because the workspace stays in one consistent pre-write state after any failure.
249. 
250. ## Result shape
251. 
252. ### `multi_file_read`
253. 
254. `multi_file_read` should optimize for readability, not JSON structure. The model needs to consume source text directly, and JSON escaping would make that harder.
255. 
256. Suggested `Content` format:
257. 
258. ```text
259. [multi_file_read summary] requested=3 succeeded=2 failed=1
260. 
261. === FILE: /repo/a.go ===
262. [indent: tab]
263.      1	package main
264.      2	
265.      3	func a() {}
266. [end file]
267. 
268. === FILE: /repo/b.go ===
269. [indent: 2 spaces]
270.     40	foo: bar
271.     41	baz: qux
272. [File truncated: showing lines 40-41 of 120. Use multi_file_read or read_file with a narrower range for more.]
273. [end file]
274. 
275. === ERROR: /repo/missing.go ===
276. error reading file: open /repo/missing.go: no such file or directory
277. [end error]
278. 
279. === FILE: /repo/too-many.go ===
280. [skipped: combined output limit reached; split into a smaller batch]
281. [end file]
282. ```
283. 
284. Design constraints:
285. 
286. - keep the existing numbered-line format unchanged inside each file block
287. - use deterministic file/error delimiters
288. - preserve current metadata headers when available
289. - keep output order identical to input order
290. - include explicit skipped markers when later files were omitted due to output budget
291. 
292. This makes the output easy for the LLM to read and easy to copy from into `multi_file_edit`.
293. 
294. ### `multi_file_edit`
295. 
296. The current tool framework returns `Result.Content string`, not a native structured payload. So the tool should return a stable JSON string in `Content`, with a short human-readable summary duplicated inside that JSON.
297. 
298. Suggested `Content` payload:
299. 
300. ```json
301. {
302.   "summary": "Requested 5 files: 0 written, 1 failed, 4 skipped by atomic mode",
303.   "mode": "atomic",
304.   "applied": false,
305.   "planned_files": 4,
306.   "written_files": 0,
307.   "failed_files": 1,
308.   "skipped_files": 4,
309.   "written_paths": [],
310.   "failed_paths": ["/repo/b.go"],
311.   "skipped_paths": ["/repo/a.go", "/repo/c.go", "/repo/d.go", "/repo/e.go"],
312.   "results": [
313.     {
314.       "path": "/repo/a.go",
315.       "status": "planned",
316.       "applied_edit_count": 2
317.     },
318.     {
319.       "path": "/repo/b.go",
320.       "status": "error",
321.       "error": "edits[1]: old_text not found in file"
322.     }
323.   ]
324. }
325. ```
326. 
327. `results[]` order should match input order.
328. 
329. Status meanings:
330. 
331. - `success`: file was written
332. - `planned`: file would have succeeded, but atomic mode skipped writes after another failure
333. - `skipped`: file was not attempted or not written because the tool stopped before execution
334. - `error`: file planning or writing failed
335. 
336. This JSON-in-`Content` approach gives the LLM machine-stable fields without requiring a change to the current tool result contract.
337. 
338. ### `IsError` semantics
339. 
340. `Result.IsError` must be defined explicitly:
341. 
342. - `multi_file_read`
343.   - `IsError = true` only for top-level malformed requests that produce no useful per-file output, such as invalid JSON, duplicate cleaned paths, or static batch-limit violations.
344.   - `IsError = false` for mixed read outcomes, including when some files fail or some later files are skipped due to runtime output limits.
345. - `multi_file_edit`
346.   - `IsError = false` only when every requested file is written successfully.
347.   - `IsError = true` in atomic mode if any file fails and no writes are applied.
348.   - `IsError = true` in `partial_success` mode if any requested file fails, even if some files were already written.
349. 
350. This preserves useful payloads while still signaling to the agent that user-visible recovery is required.
351. 
352. ## Error handling
353. 
354. ### `multi_file_read`
355. 
356. Per-file read failures should be reported inline in the output and should reuse existing `read_file` style error text where possible, such as:
357. 
358. - path not allowed by sandbox policy
359. - error accessing file
360. - error reading file
361. - file too large
362. - unsupported extracted content failure
363. 
364. Top-level validation should still fail fast for malformed requests, such as duplicate paths or batch-limit violations.
365. 
366. ### `multi_file_edit`
367. 
368. Per-file failures should reuse current single-file diagnostics, including:
369. 
370. - `old_text not found`
371. - non-unique matches
372. - overlapping edits
373. - read/write errors
374. - sandbox denial
375. 
376. This is important because agents already learned those strings and recovery patterns.
377. 
378. Top-level validation failures should fail fast before any writes if the request itself is malformed, such as:
379. 
380. - duplicate `path`
381. - empty `files`
382. - invalid JSON shape
383. - unsupported `mode`
384. - request exceeds batch limits
385. 
386. ## Registration and UX
387. 
388. - Add `MultiFileRead` and `MultiFileEdit` to `internal/tool/builtin.go`.
389. - Register `MultiFileRead` with the other file read/search tools and `MultiFileEdit` with the other mutating file tools so the built-in tool set remains easy to reason about.
390. - `multi_file_read` description should explicitly recommend:
391.   - requesting only the files and ranges needed for the current task
392.   - keeping ranges narrow on large files
393.   - copying numbered lines directly into `multi_file_edit`
394. - `multi_file_edit` description should explicitly recommend:
395.   - reading files first with `multi_file_read` or `read_file`
396.   - copying numbered lines as anchors
397.   - grouping all edits for the same file together
398.   - keeping batches small
399.   - relying on the default atomic mode unless mixed success is explicitly acceptable
400. - Parameter descriptions should mirror the proven language from `edit_file` / `multi_edit_file`.
401. 
402. ## Agent, permission, and multi-agent integration
403. 
404. The design must also specify the integration-layer work needed beyond implementing the tools themselves.
405. 
406. ### Tool registration
407. 
408. - Add `MultiFileRead{SandboxCheck: sandboxFor("multi_file_read")}` and `MultiFileEdit{SandboxCheck: sandboxFor("multi_file_edit")}` to `internal/tool/builtin.go`.
409. - Update any built-in tool documentation or release notes that enumerate file tools.
410. - Update config/documentation examples for `tool_permissions` so the new tool names are visible to users.
411. 
412. ### Permission classification
413. 
414. The permission layer currently classifies file tools by name and extracts only a single path from tool input. That is insufficient for batched file tools.
415. 
416. Implementation must:
417. 
418. - classify `multi_file_read` as a read-only file tool in plan mode
419. - classify `multi_file_edit` as a write file tool
420. - extend the permission path extractor to understand `files[].path`
````

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 15494m 23s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

**已经补上了，文档现在基本到 implementation-ready 了。** 重点补了你刚提的接入层细节，尤其是 **tool 注册、权限分类、diff/checkpoint 接入、以及多 agent 的 workingDir 隔离**。

这次新增/明确的关键点：
- **协议钉死**：绝对路径、`filepath.Clean`、`IsError` 语义、`multi_file_read` 超限时的 skipped 标记、`multi_file_edit` 的 `planned/written/failed/skipped` 计数。
- **真正闭环**：明确 `multi_file_edit` 要自动剥掉 `multi_file_read` 的 wrapper 行，这样从读到写可以直接复制锚点。
- **权限层接入**：文档已明确要扩展 `isFileTool` / `isWriteFileTool` / `IsReadOnlyTool` / 路径提取逻辑，支持 `files[].path`，不然 plan/auto/bypass 模式都不完整。
- **agent 接入**：明确了 `multi_file_edit` 不能绕过当前只给 `edit_file` / `write_file` 做的 diff preview 和 checkpoint 流程，需要扩成 multi-file 版本。
- **workingDir / 多 agent 隔离**：文档里已经写死这两个新工具应当是**无 `WorkingDir` 的无状态工具**，不设置 `SuggestedWorkingDir`，因此不会互相覆盖；如果以后有人给它们加 `WorkingDir`，必须实现 `Clone()` 并纳入 `TestAllWorkingDirToolsImplementCloner`。

我现在的判断是：**设计已经完整闭环，且把最容易在实现时漏掉的系统接入点都写清楚了。**  
如果还想再继续优化，剩下更多是工程组织层面的，比如把这份文档再拆成：
1. tool protocol spec  
2. agent/permission integration checklist  
3. implementation task breakdown  

但从“能不能直接指导实现”这个标准看，现在已经可以了。

````
1. # Multi-file read/edit tool design
2. 
3. ## Summary
4. 
5. Add two new built-in tools:
6. 
7. - `multi_file_read` for batching reads across multiple existing files in one tool call
8. - `multi_file_edit` for batching edits across multiple existing files in one tool call
9. 
10. The first version is intentionally narrow:
11. 
12. - Only works on existing files.
13. - Reuses current `read_file`, `edit_file`, and `multi_edit_file` semantics as much as possible.
14. - Uses grouped `files[]` inputs for both read and edit tools.
15. - Defaults to **atomic** cross-file behavior for easier LLM recovery.
16. - Optionally supports `mode: "partial_success"` for callers that prefer best-effort writes.
17. 
18. This keeps the tools easy for weaker models to use while avoiding a brand-new matching model.
19. 
20. ## Goals
21. 
22. - Reduce round trips when one task requires coordinated edits in several files.
23. - Reuse the already-hardened matching behavior from `edit_file` / `multi_edit_file`, including:
24.   - read_file numbered-line anchors
25.   - wrapper-line stripping
26.   - indentation normalization
27.   - CRLF tolerance
28.   - leading-indent shift fallback
29. - Preserve the same line-numbered read format that powers the current edit workflow.
30. - Return structured per-file results so the agent can retry only failures.
31. - Keep the tool request small and predictable enough for weaker models to call reliably.
32. - Provide a complete multi-file read + write loop rather than only optimizing the write side.
33. 
34. ## Non-goals
35. 
36. - Creating new files.
37. - Overwriting files wholesale like `write_file`.
38. - Replacing `read_file` / `edit_file` for single-file use.
39. - Supporting repeated `path` entries in one call.
40. - Changing single-file edit semantics.
41. 
42. ## End-to-end workflow
43. 
44. The intended LLM workflow is:
45. 
46. 1. Use `multi_file_read` to fetch the relevant files or file ranges in one round trip.
47. 2. Inspect the numbered output and decide what to change.
48. 3. Use `multi_file_edit` with numbered `old_text` anchors copied from `multi_file_read`.
49. 4. If edit planning fails, re-read only the failed files or narrower ranges and retry.
50. 
51. The two tools are designed as a pair. `multi_file_read` optimizes context collection; `multi_file_edit` optimizes coordinated writes.
52. 
53. ## Protocol clarifications
54. 
55. These points must be fixed in the design so implementation and agent behavior do not drift:
56. 
57. - `multi_file_read` and `multi_file_edit` both require absolute paths.
58. - Paths must be normalized with `filepath.Clean` before duplicate detection, sandbox checks, reads, writes, and reporting.
59. - Duplicate-path checks operate on the cleaned absolute path string.
60. - v1 does not attempt symlink canonicalization beyond `filepath.Clean`; callers should not provide the same target through multiple symlink aliases.
61. - `multi_file_edit` only supports plain text files that can be written back directly. It does not support editing extracted document formats such as PDF, DOCX, or EPUB.
62. - `multi_file_read` may support text files plus extracted document text, but only its plain text output is valid input to `multi_file_edit`.
63. - To preserve workflow parity, `multi_file_edit` matching must auto-strip `multi_file_read` wrapper lines if they are accidentally pasted into `old_text` or `new_text`. This includes:
64.   - `[multi_file_read summary] ...`
65.   - `=== FILE: ... ===`
66.   - `=== ERROR: ... ===`
67.   - `[end file]`
68.   - `[end error]`
69.   - `[skipped: ...]`
70. 
71. ## Proposed `multi_file_read` shape
72. 
73. ```json
74. {
75.   "files": [
76.     {
77.       "path": "/repo/a.go",
78.       "offset": 1,
79.       "limit": 120
80.     },
81.     {
82.       "path": "/repo/b.go",
83.       "offset": 40,
84.       "limit": 80
85.     }
86.   ],
87.   "description": "Optional UI label"
88. }
89. ```
90. 
91. Rules:
92. 
93. - `files[].path` must be unique within one call.
94. - `offset` and `limit` follow the same semantics as `read_file`.
95. - Omitted `offset` means start from the beginning.
96. - Omitted `limit` means use the multi-file default per-file cap rather than the larger single-file cap.
97. - v1 should focus on text files and extracted document text. Image/binary batching is out of scope.
98. 
99. ## Proposed `multi_file_edit` shape
100. 
101. ```json
102. {
103.   "mode": "atomic",
104.   "files": [
105.     {
106.       "path": "/repo/a.go",
107.       "edits": [
108.         {
109.           "old_text": "     12\tfoo()",
110.           "new_text": "     12\tbar()"
111.         }
112.       ]
113.     },
114.     {
115.       "path": "/repo/b.go",
116.       "edits": [
117.         {
118.           "old_text": "x := 1",
119.           "new_text": "x := 2"
120.         }
121.       ]
122.     }
123.   ],
124.   "description": "Optional UI label"
125. }
126. ```
127. 
128. Rules:
129. 
130. - `files[].path` must be unique within one call.
131. - Each file's `edits` behaves exactly like `multi_edit_file`.
132. - If the same file needs several edits, they must be grouped into one `files[i].edits`.
133. - `mode` defaults to `"atomic"` when omitted.
134. - `mode: "partial_success"` is opt-in and should be used only when the caller is prepared to reconcile mixed file state.
135. 
136. ## Limits
137. 
138. The tools should be optimized for small, coordinated multi-file changes rather than giant repository-wide rewrites.
139. 
140. ### `multi_file_read` limits
141. 
142. Recommended v1 limits:
143. 
144. - at most 20 files per call
145. - default per-file cap: 200 lines when `limit` is omitted
146. - max explicit per-file `limit`: 500 lines
147. - max combined output: 4000 lines or 300 KB, whichever comes first
148. 
149. Static request-limit violations should fail fast and tell the model to split the read into smaller batches or narrower ranges.
150. 
151. This is intentionally stricter than `read_file`. The point of `multi_file_read` is efficient context gathering, not dumping large files wholesale.
152. 
153. ### `multi_file_edit` limits
154. 
155. Recommended v1 limits:
156. 
157. - at most 10 files per call
158. - at most 20 edits per file
159. - at most 200 total edits per call
160. - at most 200 KB combined `old_text` + `new_text` payload
161. 
162. Requests beyond those limits should fail fast with a clear message telling the model to split the batch.
163. 
164. ## Execution model
165. 
166. Implementation should be split into three layers.
167. 
168. ### 1. Shared single-file reader
169. 
170. Extract the core reading logic from `read_file` into a shared helper that:
171. 
172. - validates sandbox access
173. - reads the file
174. - preserves the current line-numbered output style
175. - preserves metadata hints such as indent or encoding headers
176. - applies the same range logic as `read_file`
177. - returns either:
178.   - a formatted read block
179.   - or a per-file read error
180. 
181. This helper must be reused by:
182. 
183. - `read_file`
184. - `multi_file_read`
185. 
186. That keeps output shape consistent so the model can copy numbered lines from either tool into edit calls.
187. 
188. ### 2. Shared single-file planner
189. 
190. Extract the core planning logic from `multi_edit_file` into a shared helper that:
191. 
192. - reads original content
193. - resolves every edit against that original content
194. - validates uniqueness and overlap
195. - computes the final new content
196. - returns either:
197.   - a write plan (`path`, `new_content`, `applied_edit_count`)
198.   - or a structured failure
199. 
200. This helper must be reused by:
201. 
202. - `multi_edit_file`
203. - `multi_file_edit`
204. 
205. That keeps matching behavior consistent and avoids future drift.
206. 
207. ### 3. Multi-file orchestrators
208. 
209. #### `multi_file_read`
210. 
211. `multi_file_read` should:
212. 
213. 1. Validate top-level input before reading:
214.    - non-empty `files`
215.    - unique `path`
216.    - request stays within batch limits
217. 2. For each file:
218.    - run the shared single-file reader
219.    - append either a success block or an error block
220. 3. If the runtime combined output limit is reached:
221.    - stop appending further file bodies
222.    - append explicit skipped markers for the remaining paths
223.    - append a clear split-batch notice in the summary
224. 
225. Unlike `multi_file_edit`, `multi_file_read` should always be best-effort per file. Read failures in one path should not block the others, because partial read success is still useful for planning.
226. 
227. #### `multi_file_edit`
228. 
229. `multi_file_edit` should:
230. 
231. 1. Validate top-level input before any write:
232.    - non-empty `files`
233.    - unique `path`
234.    - no empty `old_text`
235.    - sandbox permission for every path
236.    - request stays within batch limits
237. 2. For each file:
238.    - read file
239.    - run the shared planner
240.    - store either a pending write or a failure result
241. 3. If `mode == "atomic"`:
242.    - abort all writes if any file planning step failed
243.    - return all per-file failures plus any planned successes as skipped
244. 4. If `mode == "partial_success"`:
245.    - persist only the successful pending writes
246.    - return a mixed result covering both successes and failures
247. 
248. This default matters for LLM efficiency. Atomic mode is easier for models to recover from because the workspace stays in one consistent pre-write state after any failure.
249. 
250. ## Result shape
251. 
252. ### `multi_file_read`
253. 
254. `multi_file_read` should optimize for readability, not JSON structure. The model needs to consume source text directly, and JSON escaping would make that harder.
255. 
256. Suggested `Content` format:
257. 
258. ```text
259. [multi_file_read summary] requested=3 succeeded=2 failed=1
260. 
261. === FILE: /repo/a.go ===
262. [indent: tab]
263.      1	package main
264.      2	
265.      3	func a() {}
266. [end file]
267. 
268. === FILE: /repo/b.go ===
269. [indent: 2 spaces]
270.     40	foo: bar
271.     41	baz: qux
272. [File truncated: showing lines 40-41 of 120. Use multi_file_read or read_file with a narrower range for more.]
273. [end file]
274. 
275. === ERROR: /repo/missing.go ===
276. error reading file: open /repo/missing.go: no such file or directory
277. [end error]
278. 
279. === FILE: /repo/too-many.go ===
280. [skipped: combined output limit reached; split into a smaller batch]
281. [end file]
282. ```
283. 
284. Design constraints:
285. 
286. - keep the existing numbered-line format unchanged inside each file block
287. - use deterministic file/error delimiters
288. - preserve current metadata headers when available
289. - keep output order identical to input order
290. - include explicit skipped markers when later files were omitted due to output budget
291. 
292. This makes the output easy for the LLM to read and easy to copy from into `multi_file_edit`.
293. 
294. ### `multi_file_edit`
295. 
296. The current tool framework returns `Result.Content string`, not a native structured payload. So the tool should return a stable JSON string in `Content`, with a short human-readable summary duplicated inside that JSON.
297. 
298. Suggested `Content` payload:
299. 
300. ```json
301. {
302.   "summary": "Requested 5 files: 0 written, 1 failed, 4 skipped by atomic mode",
303.   "mode": "atomic",
304.   "applied": false,
305.   "planned_files": 4,
306.   "written_files": 0,
307.   "failed_files": 1,
308.   "skipped_files": 4,
309.   "written_paths": [],
310.   "failed_paths": ["/repo/b.go"],
311.   "skipped_paths": ["/repo/a.go", "/repo/c.go", "/repo/d.go", "/repo/e.go"],
312.   "results": [
313.     {
314.       "path": "/repo/a.go",
315.       "status": "planned",
316.       "applied_edit_count": 2
317.     },
318.     {
319.       "path": "/repo/b.go",
320.       "status": "error",
321.       "error": "edits[1]: old_text not found in file"
322.     }
323.   ]
324. }
325. ```
326. 
327. `results[]` order should match input order.
328. 
329. Status meanings:
330. 
331. - `success`: file was written
332. - `planned`: file would have succeeded, but atomic mode skipped writes after another failure
333. - `skipped`: file was not attempted or not written because the tool stopped before execution
334. - `error`: file planning or writing failed
335. 
336. This JSON-in-`Content` approach gives the LLM machine-stable fields without requiring a change to the current tool result contract.
337. 
338. ### `IsError` semantics
339. 
340. `Result.IsError` must be defined explicitly:
341. 
342. - `multi_file_read`
343.   - `IsError = true` only for top-level malformed requests that produce no useful per-file output, such as invalid JSON, duplicate cleaned paths, or static batch-limit violations.
344.   - `IsError = false` for mixed read outcomes, including when some files fail or some later files are skipped due to runtime output limits.
345. - `multi_file_edit`
346.   - `IsError = false` only when every requested file is written successfully.
347.   - `IsError = true` in atomic mode if any file fails and no writes are applied.
348.   - `IsError = true` in `partial_success` mode if any requested file fails, even if some files were already written.
349. 
350. This preserves useful payloads while still signaling to the agent that user-visible recovery is required.
351. 
352. ## Error handling
353. 
354. ### `multi_file_read`
355. 
356. Per-file read failures should be reported inline in the output and should reuse existing `read_file` style error text where possible, such as:
357. 
358. - path not allowed by sandbox policy
359. - error accessing file
360. - error reading file
361. - file too large
362. - unsupported extracted content failure
363. 
364. Top-level validation should still fail fast for malformed requests, such as duplicate paths or batch-limit violations.
365. 
366. ### `multi_file_edit`
367. 
368. Per-file failures should reuse current single-file diagnostics, including:
369. 
370. - `old_text not found`
371. - non-unique matches
372. - overlapping edits
373. - read/write errors
374. - sandbox denial
375. 
376. This is important because agents already learned those strings and recovery patterns.
377. 
378. Top-level validation failures should fail fast before any writes if the request itself is malformed, such as:
379. 
380. - duplicate `path`
381. - empty `files`
382. - invalid JSON shape
383. - unsupported `mode`
384. - request exceeds batch limits
385. 
386. ## Registration and UX
387. 
388. - Add `MultiFileRead` and `MultiFileEdit` to `internal/tool/builtin.go`.
389. - Register `MultiFileRead` with the other file read/search tools and `MultiFileEdit` with the other mutating file tools so the built-in tool set remains easy to reason about.
390. - `multi_file_read` description should explicitly recommend:
391.   - requesting only the files and ranges needed for the current task
392.   - keeping ranges narrow on large files
393.   - copying numbered lines directly into `multi_file_edit`
394. - `multi_file_edit` description should explicitly recommend:
395.   - reading files first with `multi_file_read` or `read_file`
396.   - copying numbered lines as anchors
397.   - grouping all edits for the same file together
398.   - keeping batches small
399.   - relying on the default atomic mode unless mixed success is explicitly acceptable
400. - Parameter descriptions should mirror the proven language from `edit_file` / `multi_edit_file`.
401. 
402. ## Agent, permission, and multi-agent integration
403. 
404. The design must also specify the integration-layer work needed beyond implementing the tools themselves.
405. 
406. ### Tool registration
407. 
408. - Add `MultiFileRead{SandboxCheck: sandboxFor("multi_file_read")}` and `MultiFileEdit{SandboxCheck: sandboxFor("multi_file_edit")}` to `internal/tool/builtin.go`.
409. - Update any built-in tool documentation or release notes that enumerate file tools.
410. - Update config/documentation examples for `tool_permissions` so the new tool names are visible to users.
411. 
412. ### Permission classification
413. 
414. The permission layer currently classifies file tools by name and extracts only a single path from tool input. That is insufficient for batched file tools.
415. 
416. Implementation must:
417. 
418. - classify `multi_file_read` as a read-only file tool in plan mode
419. - classify `multi_file_edit` as a write file tool
420. - extend the permission path extractor to understand `files[].path`
````

</details>

1. package tool
2. 
3. import (
4. 	"context"
5. 	"encoding/json"
6. 	"fmt"
7. 	"os"
8. 	"path/filepath"
9. 	"strings"
10. 
11. 	"github.com/topcheer/ggcode/internal/extract"
12. 	"github.com/topcheer/ggcode/internal/image"
13. 	"github.com/topcheer/ggcode/internal/provider"
14. )
15. 
16. // AllowedPathChecker is a function that checks if a path is allowed by sandbox policy.
17. type AllowedPathChecker func(path string) bool
18. 
19. // ReadFile implements the read_file tool.
20. type ReadFile struct {
21. 	SandboxCheck AllowedPathChecker
22. }
23. 
24. func (t ReadFile) Name() string { return "read_file" }
25. 
26. func (t ReadFile) Description() string {
27. 	return "Read file contents into the agent's context. ALWAYS read a file with this tool before editing it. " +
28. 		"Output is line-numbered using `cat -n` format (\"   42\\t<line content>\"). Recommended workflow: copy those numbered lines directly into edit_file/multi_edit_file old_text, because the edit tools understand the prefixes and use them as anchors for single-line edits and duplicate text. " +
29. 		"Use offset/limit for files larger than ~2000 lines. " +
30. 		"Supports text, images (png/jpg/gif/webp), PDF, Office (docx/xlsx/pptx), OpenDocument (odt/ods/odp), EPUB, RTF, archives (zip/tar/tar.gz/tar.bz2), iWork, and SVG."
31. }
32. 
33. func (t ReadFile) Parameters() json.RawMessage {
34. 	return json.RawMessage(`{
35. 	"type": "object",
36. 	"properties": {
37. 		"path": {
38. 			"type": "string",
39. 			"description": "Absolute path to the file to read."
40. 		},
41. 		"offset": {
42. 			"type": "integer",
43. 			"description": "1-based line number to start reading from. Only applies to text files and extracted documents."
44. 		},
45. 		"limit": {
46. 			"type": "integer",
47. 			"description": "Maximum number of lines to read. Output is capped at 2000 lines when no limit is supplied."
48. 		},
49. 		"description": {
50. 			"type": "string",
51. 			"description": "Optional. Brief activity label shown in the UI in the user's language."
52. 		}
53. 	},
54. 	"required": [
55. 		"path"
56. 	]
57. }`)
58. }
59. 
60. const maxFileSize = 10 * 1024 * 1024 // 10MB pre-check limit
61. 
62. func (t ReadFile) Execute(ctx context.Context, input json.RawMessage) (Result, error) {
63. 	var args struct {
64. 		Path   string `json:"path"`
65. 		Offset int    `json:"offset"`
66. 		Limit  int    `json:"limit"`
67. 	}
68. 	if err := json.Unmarshal(input, &args); err != nil {
69. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("invalid input: %v", err)}, nil
70. 	}
71. 
72. 	if t.SandboxCheck != nil && !t.SandboxCheck(args.Path) {
73. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: "Error: path not allowed by sandbox policy"}, nil
74. 	}
75. 
76. 	// Pre-check file size
77. 	info, err := os.Stat(args.Path)
78. 	if err != nil {
79. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error accessing file: %v", err)}, nil
80. 	}
81. 	if info.Size() > maxFileSize {
82. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("file too large (%d MB). Use read_file with offset/limit for range reading.", info.Size()/(1024*1024))}, nil
83. 	}
84. 
85. 	data, err := os.ReadFile(args.Path)
86. 	if err != nil {
87. 		return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error reading file: %v", err)}, nil
88. 	}
89. 
90. 	// Image file handling: decode and return as multimodal result (ignores offset/limit).
91. 	if image.IsImageFile(args.Path) {
92. 		img, err := image.Decode(data)
93. 		if err != nil {
94. 			return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error decoding image: %v", err)}, nil
95. 		}
96. 		b64 := image.EncodeBase64(img)
97. 		return Result{
98. 			Content: image.Placeholder(filepath.Base(args.Path), img),
99. 			Images: []ResultImage{{
100. 				MIME:       img.MIME,
101. 				Base64:     b64,
102. 				Width:      img.Width,
103. 				Height:     img.Height,
104. 				SourcePath: args.Path,
105. 			}},
106. 		}, nil
107. 	}
108. 
109. 	// Document file handling: extract text, then apply range reading.
110. 	if extract.IsDocumentFile(args.Path) {
111. 		result, err := extract.Extract(args.Path, data)
112. 		if err != nil {
113. 			return Result{IsError: true, Content: fmt.Sprintf("error extracting document text: %v", err)}, nil
114. 		}
115. 
116. 		var header string
117. 		if result.Pages > 0 {
118. 			header = fmt.Sprintf("[Extracted from %s, %d pages]\n", result.Format, result.Pages)
119. 		} else {
120. 			header = fmt.Sprintf("[Extracted from %s]\n", result.Format)
121. 		}
122. 
123. 		text := readFileRange(result.Text, args.Offset, args.Limit, 0)
124. 		return Result{Content: header + text}, nil
125. 	}
126. 
127. 	// Plain text file: apply range reading.
128. 	content := string(data)
129. 	text := readFileRange(content, args.Offset, args.Limit, 0)
130. 
131. 	// Detect indentation style and encoding, prepend as metadata hint.
132. 	// This helps the LLM produce correct old_text matches for edit_file.
133. 	var meta string
134. 	indentHint := detectIndentStyle(content)
135. 	if indentHint != "" {
136. 		meta = indentHint
137. 	}
138. 	encHint := detectEncoding(data)
139. 	if encHint != "" {
140. 		if meta != "" {
141. 			meta += ", "
142. 		}
143. 		meta += encHint
144. 	}
145. 	if meta != "" {
146. 		text = "[" + meta + "]\n" + text
147. 	}
148. 
149. 	return Result{Content: text}, nil
150. }
151. 
152. // detectIndentStyle analyzes the first ~200 lines to determine tab vs space indentation.
153. func detectIndentStyle(content string) string {
154. 	tabs, spaces := 0, 0
155. 	lines := strings.Split(content, "\n")
156. 	limit := len(lines)
157. 	if limit > 200 {
158. 		limit = 200
159. 	}
160. 	for _, line := range lines[:limit] {
161. 		if len(line) == 0 || (line[0] != ' ' && line[0] != '\t') {
162. 			continue
163. 		}
164. 		if line[0] == '\t' {
165. 			tabs++
166. 		} else {
167. 			i := 0
168. 			for i < len(line) && line[i] == ' ' {
169. 				i++
170. 			}
171. 			if i >= 2 {
172. 				spaces++
173. 			}
174. 		}
175. 	}
176. 	total := tabs + spaces
177. 	if total == 0 {
178. 		return ""
179. 	}
180. 	if tabs > spaces {
181. 		return "indent: tab"
182. 	}
183. 	// Detect base indent unit by finding the greatest common divisor
184. 	// of all leading-space counts. This handles 2-space YAML vs 4-space Python/etc.
185. 	widths := map[int]int{}
186. 	for _, line := range lines[:limit] {
187. 		if len(line) == 0 || line[0] != ' ' {
188. 			continue
189. 		}
190. 		i := 0
191. 		for i < len(line) && line[i] == ' ' {
192. 			i++
193. 		}
194. 		if i >= 2 {
195. 			widths[i]++
196. 		}
197. 	}
198. 	if len(widths) == 0 {
199. 		return "indent: spaces"
200. 	}
201. 	// Find GCD of all observed widths
202. 	allWidths := make([]int, 0, len(widths))
203. 	for w := range widths {
204. 		allWidths = append(allWidths, w)
205. 	}
206. 	g := allWidths[0]
207. 	for _, w := range allWidths[1:] {
208. 		g = gcd(g, w)
209. 	}
210. 	if g < 2 {
211. 		g = 2
212. 	}
213. 	return fmt.Sprintf("indent: %d spaces", g)
214. }
215. 
216. func gcd(a, b int) int {
217. 	for b != 0 {
218. 		a, b = b, a%b
219. 	}
220. 	return a
221. }
222. 
223. // detectEncoding checks for BOM or non-UTF-8 content.
224. func detectEncoding(data []byte) string {
225. 	if len(data) >= 3 && data[0] == 0xEF && data[1] == 0xBB && data[2] == 0xBF {
226. 		return "encoding: UTF-8 BOM"
227. 	}
228. 	for _, b := range data[:min(len(data), 512)] {
229. 		if b == 0 {
230. 			return "encoding: binary/UTF-16"
231. 		}
232. 	}
233. 	return ""
234. }
235. 
236. // --- provider.ToolDefinition helper ---
237. 
238. // ToolDef creates a provider.ToolDefinition from a Tool.
239. func ToolDef(t Tool) provider.ToolDefinition {
240. 	return provider.ToolDefinition{
241. 		Name:        t.Name(),
242. 		Description: t.Description(),
243. 		Parameters:  t.Parameters(),
244. 	}
245. }
246.
```
